abstract:
The widespread adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) has enabled diverse
applications with very different latency requirements. Existing LLM serving
frameworks rely on siloed infrastructure with coarse-grained workload
segregation -- interactive and batch -- leading to inefficient resource
utilization and limited support for fine-grained Quality-of-Service (QoS)
differentiation. This results in operational inefficiencies, over-provisioning
and poor load management during traffic surges.
We present Niyama, a novel QoS-driven inference serving system that enables
efficient co-scheduling of diverse workloads on shared infrastructure. Niyama
introduces fine-grained QoS classification allowing applications to specify
precise latency requirements, and dynamically adapts scheduling decisions based
on real-time system state. Leveraging the predictable execution characteristics
of LLM inference, Niyama implements a dynamic chunking mechanism to improve
overall throughput while maintaining strict QoS guarantees. Additionally,
Niyama employs a hybrid prioritization policy that balances fairness and
efficiency, and employs selective request relegation that enables graceful
service degradation during overload conditions. Our evaluation demonstrates
that Niyama increases serving capacity by 32% compared to current siloed
deployments, while maintaining QoS guarantees. Notably, under extreme load, our
system reduces SLO violations by an order of magnitude compared to current
strategies.

keyInsights: Predictable execution characteristics of LLM inference enable effective dynamic scheduling
Balancing fairness and efficiency through hybrid prioritization improves overall performance
Selective request dropping during overload maintains stability for majority of workloads
originalProblem: Current LLM serving frameworks use siloed infrastructure leading to GPU underutilization
Existing systems lack fine-grained QoS differentiation between workloads
Poor handling of traffic surges and overload conditions with simple FCFS scheduling
results: 32% increase in serving capacity while maintaining QoS guarantees
Order of magnitude reduction in SLO violations under extreme load
1.5x to 2.4x higher goodput compared to Sarathi-FCFS baseline
13-32% reduction in GPU requirements versus siloed deployments
solution: QoS-aware adaptive scheduling algorithm that dynamically adjusts chunk sizes
Hybrid prioritization policy combining EDF and SJF scheduling approaches
Eager relegation mechanism for graceful service degradation under extreme load
paper_summary.summary: Microsoft Research India introduces Niyama, a QoS-driven LLM inference serving system that enables efficient co-scheduling of diverse workloads on shared infrastructure, increasing serving capacity by 32% while reducing GPU requirements by 13-32% compared to siloed deployments through adaptive scheduling and hybrid prioritization techniques.

title: Niyama : Breaking the Silos of LLM Inference Serving

abstract: Large multimodal models (LMMs) demonstrate impressive capabilities in understanding images, videos, and audio beyond text. However, efficiently serving LMMs in production environments poses significant challenges due to their complex architectures and heterogeneous characteristics across their multi-stage inference pipelines. We present the first comprehensive systems analysis of two prominent LMM architectures, decoder-only and cross-attention, across six representative open-source models, revealing key systems design implications. We also present an in-depth analysis of production LMM inference traces, uncovering unique workload characteristics, including variable, heavy-tailed request distributions and bursty traffic patterns. Based on these insights, we propose ModServe, a modular LMM serving system that decouples stages for independent optimization and adaptive scaling. ModServe dynamically reconfigures stages and handles bursty traffic with modality-aware scheduling and autoscaling to meet tail latency SLOs while minimizing costs. ModServe achieves 3.3-5.5x higher throughput (leading to 25-41.3% cost saving) while meeting SLOs on a 128-GPU cluster with production traces.

keyInsights: A comprehensive LMM characterization study, including production traces, revealed that image encoding is a major bottleneck for TTFT, consuming up to 79% of the latency for some cross-attention models.

Different LMM inference stages and architectures exhibit distinct computational characteristics and optimal configurations (batching, parallelism), indicating that monolithic serving approaches are inefficient.

Production LMM traffic is highly dynamic and "image-driven," distinct from text-only workloads, which renders traditional text-centric traffic prediction and load balancing methods ineffective, leading to overprovisioning or SLO violations.
originalProblem: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have complex, multi-stage inference pipelines with heterogeneous resource needs across stages (e.g., image encoding, LLM prefill, decode) that monolithic serving systems cannot efficiently optimize.
Image encoding, particularly for cross-attention LMMs, often acts as a significant bottleneck for Time-To-First-Token (TTFT), impacting the responsiveness of interactive applications.

Production LMM workloads exhibit high burstiness, variability, and heavy-tailed request distributions, frequently driven by image inputs, making existing text-centric traffic prediction and load balancing methods inadequate.
results:
ModServe achieved 3.3
	5.5
	 higher throughput compared to state-of-the-art monolithic serving systems (vLLM) while consistently meeting TTFT and TBT Service Level Objectives.

It reduced GPU resource usage by 25
	41.3% over monolithic systems when handling production LMM inference traces, resulting in substantial cost savings while maintaining tail latency SLOs.
The decoupled architecture, combined with modality-aware scheduling and routing, reduced average and P99 TTFT by up to 27-46% and 42-47% respectively, demonstrating improved responsiveness.
solution: ModServe proposes a modular architecture that decouples LMM inference stages (image-specific preprocessing/encoding and text-specific LLM prefill/decode) into distinct "Image Instances" and "Text Instances" for independent optimization and scaling.
It employs modality-aware mechanisms including token-aware autoscaling for instance pools, stage-aware model sharding, and stage-specific batch sizing, guided by offline LMM profile generation.

The system implements modality-aware request routing (e.g., parallelizing multi-image requests, intelligent load-based routing) and SLO-driven priority scheduling within instances to manage heterogeneous workloads and reduce tail latency.
paper_summary.summary:
ModServe implements modality- and stage-aware resource disaggregation to efficiently serve Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) in production environments. The system, validated with Azure production traces, achieves 3.3
	5.5
	 higher throughput and reduces GPU costs by 25
	41.3% compared to monolithic serving systems, effectively meeting tail latency Service Level Objectives.

title: ModServe: Modality- and Stage-Aware Resource Disaggregation for Scalable Multimodal Model Serving

abstract: Diffusion models are pivotal for generating high-quality images and videos. Inspired by the success of OpenAI's Sora, the backbone of diffusion models is evolving from U-Net to Transformer, known as Diffusion Transformers (DiTs). However, generating high-quality content necessitates longer sequence lengths, exponentially increasing the computation required for the attention mechanism, and escalating DiTs inference latency. Parallel inference is essential for real-time DiTs deployments, but relying on a single parallel method is impractical due to poor scalability at large scales. This paper introduces xDiT, a comprehensive parallel inference engine for DiTs. After thoroughly investigating existing DiTs parallel approaches, xDiT chooses Sequence Parallel (SP) and PipeFusion, a novel Patch-level Pipeline Parallel method, as intra-image parallel strategies, alongside CFG parallel for inter-image parallelism. xDiT can flexibly combine these parallel approaches in a hybrid manner, offering a robust and scalable solution. Experimental results on two 8xL40 GPUs (PCIe) nodes interconnected by Ethernet and an 8xA100 (NVLink) node showcase xDiT's exceptional scalability across five state-of-the-art DiTs. Notably, we are the first to demonstrate DiTs scalability on Ethernet-connected GPU clusters. xDiT is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: No single parallel method is universally optimal for DiTs; hybrid parallel strategies are essential for superior scalability and lower latency across diverse model architectures and heterogeneous hardware.

PipeFusion offers significant memory efficiency, consuming substantially less memory for model parameters and activations compared to other methods like SP and DistriFusion, which is crucial for large models.
Parallel VAE primarily enables the generation of previously unattainable ultra-high resolutions by mitigating OOM, rather than consistently accelerating the generation of existing resolutions.
originalProblem: High inference latency for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) when generating high-resolution content due to the quadratic scaling of self-attention with sequence length.

Existing parallel inference methods for Large Language Models are insufficient for DiTs, encountering issues like high communication overhead, significant memory demands, and inability to handle diverse DiT architectures.
Memory bottlenecks in both the Transformer backbone and the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) component lead to Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors for large models and ultra-high-resolution outputs.
results: Achieved a 13.29x speedup for 4096px Pixart image generation on 16 L40 GPUs, reducing latency from 245 seconds to 17 seconds, and was the first to demonstrate DiTs scalability on Ethernet-connected GPU clusters.

Enabled VAE decoding for images up to 7168px on L40 GPUs and 8192px on A100 GPUs through patch parallelism and temporal memory optimization, effectively overcoming memory limitations for ultra-high-resolution outputs.

Qualitative visual inspections and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) scores confirmed that xDiT's hybrid parallel approach maintains output quality, with generated images virtually indistinguishable from baseline single-device generations.
solution: Developed xDiT, a hybrid parallel inference engine that combines Sequence Parallelism (SP), a novel Patch-level Pipeline Parallelism (PipeFusion), and Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) Parallelism.
Designed a new SP method adapted for in-context conditioning in DiTs and modified SP to ensure consistent Key/Value buffers when hybridized with PipeFusion.
Implemented Patch Parallelism and temporal memory optimization for the VAE to overcome memory bottlenecks and enable decoding of extremely high-resolution images.
paper_summary.summary: xDiT is an inference engine for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) designed to accelerate high-resolution content generation through massive parallelism. It achieves up to a 13.29x speedup for 4096px image generation on 16 L40 GPUs and enables the decoding of images up to 8192px, utilizing a hybrid parallel approach across diverse hardware.

title: xDiT: an Inference Engine for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) with Massive Parallelism

abstract: Any-to-any multimodal models that jointly handle text, images, video, and audio represent a significant advance in multimodal AI. However, their complex architectures (typically combining multiple autoregressive LLMs, diffusion transformers, and other specialized components) pose substantial challenges for efficient model serving. Existing serving systems are mainly tailored to a single paradigm, such as autoregressive LLMs for text generation or diffusion transformers for visual generation. They lack support for any-to-any pipelines that involve multiple interconnected model components. As a result, developers must manually handle cross-stage interactions, leading to huge performance degradation. We present vLLM-Omni, a fully disaggregated serving system for any-to-any models. vLLM-Omni features a novel stage abstraction that enables users to decompose complex any-to-any architectures into interconnected stages represented as a graph, and a disaggregated stage execution backend that optimizes resource utilization and throughput across stages. Each stage is independently served by an LLM or diffusion engine with per-stage request batching, flexible GPU allocation, and unified inter-stage connectors for data routing. Experimental results demonstrate that vLLM-Omni reduces job completion time (JCT) by up to 91.4% compared to baseline methods. The code is public available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decomposing complex multimodal models into a graph of independently served stages, each leveraging specialized engines, dramatically improves efficiency and resource utilization.
A flexible stage abstraction simplifies the development and deployment of heterogeneous multimodal pipelines, abstracting away serving complexities.
Efficient inter-stage data transfer mechanisms with negligible overhead are crucial for maintaining performance in disaggregated architectures, even across distributed setups.
originalProblem: Existing AI serving systems are specialized for single-modality models, inefficiently handling the diverse components of "any-to-any" multimodal architectures.
Deploying complex, multi-component multimodal models currently requires manual orchestration, leading to performance degradation, inefficient resource allocation, and increased latency.
The architectural heterogeneity of models combining elements like autoregressive LLMs and Diffusion Transformers presents significant challenges for unified, high-performance serving infrastructure.
results: vLLM-Omni reduced Real-Time Factor (RTF) by up to 90.7% and Job Completion Time (JCT) by up to 91.4% for Qwen-Omni architectures compared to Hugging Face Transformers.
It achieved a 2.40x speedup for text-to-image and 3.72x speedup for image-to-image tasks on the BAGEL model, and an 11.58x speedup for text-to-speech with MiMo-Audio.
The system consistently delivered a 1.26x overall speedup for Diffusion-based models compared to Diffusers, demonstrating negligible data transfer overhead from its unified connector.
solution: The system proposes a novel "stage graph abstraction" that allows developers to decompose complex multimodal models into interconnected, modular stages with defined data transfer functions.
It implements a fully disaggregated execution backend, where each stage runs on an independent, specialized engine with tailored resource allocation and intra-stage request batching.
A unified connector facilitates seamless and low-latency data transfer between these disaggregated stages, supporting both single-node and distributed deployment topologies.
paper_summary.summary: vLLM-Omni introduces a fully disaggregated serving system for any-to-any multimodal models, enabling efficient deployment of complex AI architectures that integrate diverse components like LLMs and Diffusion Transformers. The system achieves substantial reductions in job completion time and improved throughput by abstracting models into stages served by specialized engines.

title: vLLM-Omni: Fully Disaggregated Serving for Any-to-Any Multimodal Models

abstract: Generative models are reshaping the live-streaming industry by redefining how content is created, styled, and delivered. Previous image-based streaming diffusion models have powered efficient and creative live streaming products but have hit limits on temporal consistency due to the foundation of image-based designs. Recent advances in video diffusion have markedly improved temporal consistency and sampling efficiency for offline generation. However, offline generation systems primarily optimize throughput by batching large workloads. In contrast, live online streaming operates under strict service-level objectives (SLOs): time-to-first-frame must be minimal, and every frame must meet a per-frame deadline with low jitter. Besides, scalable multi-GPU serving for real-time streams remains largely unresolved so far. To address this, we present StreamDiffusionV2, a training-free pipeline for interactive live streaming with video diffusion models. StreamDiffusionV2 integrates an SLO-aware batching scheduler and a block scheduler, together with a sink-token--guided rolling KV cache, a motion-aware noise controller, and other system-level optimizations. Moreover, we introduce a scalable pipeline orchestration that parallelizes the diffusion process across denoising steps and network layers, achieving near-linear FPS scaling without violating latency guarantees. The system scales seamlessly across heterogeneous GPU environments and supports flexible denoising steps (e.g., 1--4), enabling both ultra-low-latency and higher-quality modes. Without TensorRT or quantization, StreamDiffusionV2 renders the first frame within 0.5s and attains 58.28 FPS with a 14B-parameter model and 64.52 FPS with a 1.3B-parameter model on four H100 GPUs, making state-of-the-art generative live streaming practical and accessible--from individual creators to enterprise-scale platforms.

keyInsights: Adapting video diffusion models for real-time streaming necessitates a holistic system-algorithm co-design that addresses both inference scheduling and intrinsic model limitations.
Continuous and adaptive updates to temporal anchoring mechanisms (sink tokens, RoPE) are crucial for maintaining style and semantic consistency over unbounded, long-horizon video generation.
Dynamic workload balancing and communication overlapping across GPUs are essential for achieving scalable throughput while adhering to strict real-time latency budgets.
Future AI hardware and algorithmic trends indicate a shift towards memory-bound regimes, making designs that optimize memory traffic and exploit pipeline parallelism increasingly important.
originalProblem: Existing video diffusion models are optimized for offline throughput, leading to high "Time-to-First-Frame" and missed per-frame deadlines for interactive live streaming.
Models designed for fixed-length video clips suffer from visual drift and temporal inconsistency when generating long, continuous streams over unbounded horizons.
Current video diffusion models struggle with rapid motion, producing artifacts like blur, ghosting, or tearing due to training biases and fixed noise schedules.
Scalable multi-GPU deployment for real-time video generation is inefficient, burdened by high communication overhead and underutilization under strict latency constraints.
results: Achieved an ultra-low Time-to-First-Frame (TTFF) of 0.37s at 30 FPS (18x lower than CausVid) and 0.47s at 16 FPS on an H100 GPU, significantly outperforming baselines.
Demonstrated high throughput, reaching up to 61.57 FPS for a 1.3B-parameter model at 512x512 resolution on 4x H100 GPUs, with a low 0.2% SLO miss rate under a 1.0s latency budget.
Produced high-quality video with superior temporal consistency, achieving a Temporal CLIP Score of 98.51 and a Warp Error of 73.31, outperforming image diffusion and vanilla video-to-video baselines.
solution: An SLO-aware batching scheduler dynamically adjusts the stream batch size to optimize GPU utilization while ensuring low latency and meeting per-frame deadlines.
Adaptive sink and RoPE refresh policies continuously update temporal contexts to prevent visual drift and maintain long-term consistency in unbounded live streams.
A motion-aware noise scheduler dynamically adjusts denoising strength based on estimated motion magnitude, reducing tearing in fast-moving scenes and recovering details in slow ones.
A multi-pipeline orchestration strategy, combined with dynamic DiT block scheduling and asynchronous communication, enables efficient and scalable multi-GPU deployment for high aggregate FPS.
paper_summary.summary: StreamDiffusionV2 presents a training-free inference system that adapts state-of-the-art video diffusion models for real-time, interactive video generation. This system achieves an ultra-low Time-to-First-Frame (TTFF) of 0.37s and maintains robust performance up to 61.57 FPS on a 1.3B-parameter model with 4x H100 GPUs, ensuring high temporal consistency and adaptability to dynamic content.

title: StreamDiffusionV2: A Streaming System for Dynamic and Interactive Video Generation

abstract:
LLM inference must meet strict latency SLOs (e.g., 100 ms P99
time-between-tokens) while maximizing goodput. Yet, real-world variability in
prompt and response lengths skews compute-intensive prefill and memory-bound
decode phases, making both colocated (even with chunked prefill) and
disaggregated deployments unable to simultaneously deliver low tail latency and
high throughput.
We introduce DynaServe, a high-performance LLM serving system built atop vLLM
that unifies and extends both paradigms for maximizing goodput under SLO
constraints, when handling unbalanced and dynamic workloads. It relies on a
micro-request abstraction, which arbitrarily splits each request at any token
boundary into at most two cooperating segments. A two-level scheduling
framework then balances micro-request load across unified GPU instances. The
global scheduler rapidly selects per-request split points by considering both
the request's prefill/decode time ratio and the current load across GPU
instances. The local schedulers on each GPU instance independently form
SLO-aware batches, adjusting their composition in response to workload
fluctuations, potential latency spikes and per-GPU under/over utilization. On
real-world traces, DynaServe boosts the overall serving capacity from
1.15$\times$ to 3.07$\times$, improves goodput by up to 1.91$\times$ and
1.61$\times$, and improves the performance by up to 60\% in a hybrid workload
under SLO compared to state-of-the-art colocated and disaggregated baselines.

keyInsights: Neither purely colocated nor purely disaggregated LLM serving architectures optimally handle unbalanced and dynamic real-world workloads, indicating a need for a unified and adaptive approach.

A fine-grained 'micro-request' abstraction, allowing arbitrary splitting of LLM requests into smaller, contiguous token spans, enables greater flexibility in resource allocation and workload balancing.

A two-level dynamic scheduling framework, with a global predictive scheduler for splitting and routing and a local SLO-aware scheduler for batch composition, effectively manages prefill-decode trade-offs and minimizes interference.
originalProblem: Existing LLM serving systems struggle to simultaneously meet strict latency Service Level Objectives (SLOs) and maximize goodput under dynamic and unbalanced real-world workloads.
PD Colocation approaches (e.g., vLLM) suffer from 'prefill-decode interference,' causing severe tail latency violations, especially with long prompt contexts.

PD Disaggregation approaches (e.g., DistServe) eliminate interference but lead to significant resource under-utilization and reduced throughput due to workload imbalances between dedicated prefill and decode GPU pools.
results: DynaServe achieves up to 1.91x higher goodput over PD Colocation and up to 1.61x higher goodput over PD Disaggregation under various workloads.
It consistently provides up to 3x higher serving capacity (QPS) compared to PD Colocation and 1.37x compared to PD Disaggregation while maintaining a 100 ms P99 Time-Between-Tokens (TBT) latency SLO.
SLO-aware batching boosts SLO attainment to 99%, and the chunk-based KV cache transfer mechanism reduces non-overlapped transfer time by 94%.
solution: Introduces the 'micro-request' abstraction, allowing LLM requests to be arbitrarily split at any token boundary into two micro-requests, which can be processed on unified execution instances.

Employs a two-level dynamic scheduling framework: a global scheduler determines optimal request split points and routes micro-requests based on predicted execution times, while local schedulers compose batches on each GPU, prioritizing SLO adherence by dynamically managing prefill and decode budgets.

Integrates an efficient chunk-based Key-Value (KV) cache transfer mechanism that allows immediate, overlapped transfer of KV cache blocks between GPU instances for partitioned requests, ensuring seamless data movement.
paper_summary.summary: DynaServe presents a unified and elastic execution framework for Large Language Model serving that tackles the challenges of dynamic and unbalanced real-world workloads. It adaptively partitions and schedules LLM requests through a novel micro-request abstraction and two-level scheduling, leading to up to 1.91x higher goodput and up to 3x higher serving capacity while strictly adhering to latency Service Level Objectives.

title: DynaServe: Unified and Elastic Execution for Dynamic Disaggregated LLM  Serving

abstract: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to Autoregressive Models (ARMs), utilizing parallel decoding to overcome sequential bottlenecks. However, existing research focuses primarily on kernel-level optimizations, lacking a holistic serving framework that addresses the unique memory dynamics of diffusion processes in production. We identify a critical "memory footprint crisis" specific to dLLMs, driven by monolithic logit tensors and the severe resource oscillation between compute-bound "Refresh" phases and bandwidth-bound "Reuse" phases. To bridge this gap, we present dLLM-Serve, an efficient dLLM serving system that co-optimizes memory footprint, computational scheduling, and generation quality. dLLM-Serve introduces Logit-Aware Activation Budgeting to decompose transient tensor peaks, a Phase-Multiplexed Scheduler to interleave heterogeneous request phases, and Head-Centric Sparse Attention to decouple logical sparsity from physical storage. We evaluate dLLM-Serve on diverse workloads (LiveBench, Burst, OSC) and GPUs (RTX 4090, L40S). Relative to the state-of-the-art baseline, dLLM-Serve improves throughput by 1.61$\times$-1.81$\times$ on the consumer-grade RTX 4090 and 1.60$\times$-1.74$\times$ on the server-grade NVIDIA L40S, while reducing tail latency by nearly 4$\times$ under heavy contention. dLLM-Serve establishes the first blueprint for scalable dLLM inference, converting theoretical algorithmic sparsity into tangible wall-clock acceleration across heterogeneous hardware. The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decomposing the logit computation is highly effective in mitigating transient memory spikes, allowing for greater overall batch sizes and concurrent request handling.

Dynamic scheduling that acknowledges the heterogeneous resource demands of dLLM denoising phases can significantly improve GPU utilization and system throughput by intelligently interleaving workloads.

True memory efficiency and generation quality in sparse attention for dLLMs are achieved by supporting head-specific token retention and ensuring physically dense storage, decoupling logical sparsity from memory layout.
originalProblem: Diffusion LLMs suffer from a 'Memory Footprint Crisis' due to monolithic logit tensors during iterative decoding, causing massive transient memory spikes that limit concurrent requests.

The iterative denoising process in dLLMs leads to 'violent resource oscillation' between compute-heavy 'Refresh' and bandwidth-bound 'Reuse' phases, resulting in severe GPU underutilization with static scheduling.
Existing sparse attention methods for dLLMs either degrade generation quality due to uniform token selection or fail to reclaim physical memory, preventing true efficiency gains in serving contexts.
results: dLLM-Serve achieved up to 3.12x higher peak throughput on server-grade NVIDIA L40S and 1.81x on consumer-grade RTX 4090 compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
The system reduced average latency by nearly 4x under heavy contention and substantially compressed tail latency and jitter, providing more stable and predictable performance.

Head-Centric sparsity consistently outperformed uniform sparsity, yielding 2.5x higher pass@1 accuracy on HumanEval at 10% retention and matching higher retention baselines with half the memory footprint.
solution: Logit-Aware Activation Budgeting (Logit Decomposition) serially computes logits in sub-batches, strictly bounding peak activation memory to maximize KV Cache capacity.
A Phase-Multiplexed Greedy Scheduler dynamically admits new 'Refresh' phase requests by exploiting the released query-token headroom when other requests transition to less demanding 'Reuse' phases.

A Head-Centric Sparse KV Cache Manager computes independent per-head token importance and packs selected tokens into physically dense, contiguous memory blocks for efficient access and improved quality.
paper_summary.summary: dLLM-Serve introduces a comprehensive system design for serving Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) in production environments, optimizing memory, scheduling, and generation quality. The system achieves up to 3.12x higher throughput and nearly 4x lower latency compared to baselines on server-grade hardware, enabling scalable and stable dLLM inference.

title: Taming the Memory Footprint Crisis: System Design for Production Diffusion LLM Serving

abstract:
Diffusion-based text-to-image generation models trade latency for quality: small models are fast but generate lower-quality images, while large models produce better images but are slow.
We present MoDM, a novel caching-based serving system for diffusion models that dynamically balances latency and quality through a mixture of diffusion models. Unlike prior approaches that rely on model-specific internal features, MoDM caches final images, allowing seamless retrieval and reuse across multiple diffusion model families.
This design enables adaptive serving by dynamically balancing latency and image quality: using smaller models for cache-hit requests to reduce latency while reserving larger models for cache-miss requests to maintain quality. Small model image quality is preserved using retrieved cached images.
We design a global monitor that optimally allocates GPU resources and balances inference workload, ensuring high throughput while meeting service-level objectives under varying request rates. Our evaluations show that MoDM significantly reduces average serving time by 2.5x while retaining image quality, making it a practical solution for scalable and resource-efficient model deployment.
title: MoDM: Efficient Serving for Image Generation via Mixture-of-Diffusion Models

abstract:
Diffusion transformers have been widely adopted for text-to-image synthesis.
While scaling these models up to billions of parameters shows promise, the
effectiveness of scaling beyond current sizes remains underexplored and
challenging. By explicitly exploiting the computational heterogeneity of image
generations, we develop a new family of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models
(EC-DIT) for diffusion transformers with expert-choice routing. EC-DIT learns
to adaptively optimize the compute allocated to understand the input texts and
generate the respective image patches, enabling heterogeneous computation
aligned with varying text-image complexities. This heterogeneity provides an
efficient way of scaling EC-DIT up to 97 billion parameters and achieving
significant improvements in training convergence, text-to-image alignment, and
overall generation quality over dense models and conventional MoE models.
Through extensive ablations, we show that EC-DIT demonstrates superior
scalability and adaptive compute allocation by recognizing varying textual
importance through end-to-end training. Notably, in text-to-image alignment
evaluation, our largest models achieve a state-of-the-art GenEval score of
71.68% and still maintain competitive inference speed with intuitive
interpretability.
title: EC-DIT: Scaling Diffusion Transformers with Adaptive Expert-Choice  Routing

abstract:
The Text-to-Image (T2I) diffusion model has emerged as one of the most widely adopted generative models. However, serving diffusion models at the granularity of entire images introduces significant challenges, particularly under multi-resolution workloads. First, image-level serving obstructs batching across requests. Second, heterogeneous resolutions exhibit distinct locality characteristics, making it difficult to apply a uniform cache policy effectively.
To address these challenges, we present PatchedServe, a Patch Management Framework for SLO-Optimized Hybrid-Resolution Diffusion Serving. PatchedServe is the first SLO-optimized T2I diffusion serving framework designed to handle heterogeneous resolutions. Specifically, it incorporates a novel patch-based processing workflow that substantially improves throughput for hybrid-resolution inputs. Moreover, PatchedServe devises a patch-level cache reuse policy to fully exploit diffusion redundancies and integrates an SLO-aware scheduling algorithm with lightweight online latency prediction to improve responsiveness. Our evaluation demonstrates that PatchedServe achieves 30.1 % higher SLO satisfaction than the state-of-the-art diffusion serving system, while preserving image quality.
title: PATCHEDSERVE: A Patch Management Framework for SLO-Optimized Hybrid Resolution Diffusion Serving

abstract: Diffusion-based generative models have become dominant generators of high-fidelity images and videos but remain limited by their computationally expensive inference procedures. Existing acceleration techniques either require extensive model retraining or compromise significantly on sample quality. This paper explores a general, training-free, and model-agnostic acceleration strategy via multi-core parallelism. Our framework views multi-core diffusion sampling as an ODE solver pipeline, where slower yet accurate solvers progressively rectify faster solvers through a theoretically justified inter-core communication mechanism. This motivates our multi-core training-free diffusion sampling accelerator, CHORDS, which is compatible with various diffusion samplers, model architectures, and modalities. Through extensive experiments, CHORDS significantly accelerates sampling across diverse large-scale image and video diffusion models, yielding up to 2.1x speedup with four cores, improving by 50% over baselines, and 2.9x speedup with eight cores, all without quality degradation. This advancement enables CHORDS to establish a solid foundation for real-time, high-fidelity diffusion generation.
title: CHORDS: Diffusion Sampling Accelerator with Multi-core Hierarchical ODE Solvers

abstract:
Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models excel at generating high-quality images through iterative denoising steps, but serving them under strict Service Level Objectives (SLOs) is challenging due to their high computational cost, particularly at large resolutions. Existing serving systems use fixed degree sequence parallelism, which is inefficient for heterogeneous workloads with mixed resolutions and deadlines, leading to poor GPU utilization and low SLO attainment.
In this paper, we propose step-level sequence parallelism to dynamically adjust the degree of parallelism of individual requests according to their deadlines. We present TetriServe, a DiT serving system that implements this strategy for highly efficient image generation. Specifically, TetriServe introduces a novel round-based scheduling mechanism that improves SLO attainment: (1) discretizing time into fixed rounds to make deadline-aware scheduling tractable, (2) adapting parallelism at the step level and minimize GPU hour consumption, and (3) jointly packing requests to minimize late completions. Extensive evaluation on state-of-the-art DiT models shows that TetriServe achieves up to 32% higher SLO attainment compared to existing solutions without degrading image quality.

keyInsights: Dynamically adjusting the degree of parallelism for individual requests at the fine-grained step level can significantly improve DiT serving efficiency and SLO attainment, overcoming the limitations of static parallelism.
Optimal step-level scheduling for deadline satisfaction in DiT serving is an NP-hard problem, necessitating efficient heuristic approaches for practical online deployment.
Discretizing continuous time into fixed-duration "rounds" allows for tractable, real-time, deadline-aware GPU allocation and request packing decisions using dynamic programming.
originalProblem: DiT models are computationally expensive, making it challenging to meet strict latency Service Level Objectives (SLOs) for online image generation services.

Existing DiT serving systems use fixed sequence parallelism (SP), which is inefficient for heterogeneous workloads, leading to GPU underutilization for large requests and excessive communication overhead for small requests.
The optimal step-level scheduling to maximize deadline satisfaction under a fixed GPU budget is an NP-hard problem, requiring efficient heuristic solutions for online serving.
results: TetriServe achieves up to 32% higher SLO Attainment Ratio (SAR) compared to xDiT variants and Resolution-Specific SP baselines, particularly under tight SLOs and heterogeneous workloads.
Demonstrates robust performance across the entire spectrum of input resolutions (256x256 to 2048x2048) and under bursty traffic, while significantly improving tail latency.
The system is orthogonal and compatible with cache-based diffusion acceleration techniques, further enhancing SAR when integrated with methods like Nirvana.
solution: Introduces TetriServe, a system that employs step-level dynamic sequence parallelism, allowing the number of GPUs allocated to a request to change at each denoising step.

Developed a deadline-aware, round-based scheduler that uses a cost model and dynamic programming to determine the minimal GPU allocation for requests to meet deadlines, then packs requests to maximize survival into the next round.
Incorporates efficiency enhancements such as GPU placement preservation, work-conserving elastic scale-up for idle GPUs, and selective continuous batching for small requests.
paper_summary.summary: TetriServe, a system developed by researchers at the University of Michigan, addresses the efficient serving of Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models by dynamically adjusting GPU parallelism at the step level for heterogeneous image generation requests. It significantly improves Service Level Objective (SLO) attainment by up to 32% compared to static parallelism baselines while reducing tail latency across various resolutions and workloads.

title: TetriServe: Efficient DiT Serving for Heterogeneous Image Generation

abstract: Diffusion models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generative tasks such as image synthesis and video generation, with Transformer architectures further enhancing performance. However, the high computational cost of diffusion Transformers-stemming from a large number of sampling steps and complex per-step computations-presents significant challenges for real-time deployment. In this paper, we introduce OmniCache, a training-free acceleration method that exploits the global redundancy inherent in the denoising process. Unlike existing methods that determine caching strategies based on inter-step similarities and tend to prioritize reusing later sampling steps, our approach originates from the sampling perspective of DIT models. We systematically analyze the model's sampling trajectories and strategically distribute cache reuse across the entire sampling process. This global perspective enables more effective utilization of cached computations throughout the diffusion trajectory, rather than concentrating reuse within limited segments of the sampling procedure. In addition, during cache reuse, we dynamically estimate the corresponding noise and filter it out to reduce its impact on the sampling direction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach accelerates the sampling process while maintaining competitive generative quality, offering a promising and practical solution for efficient deployment of diffusion-based generative models.

keyInsights: Diffusion model sampling trajectories exhibit a consistent 'boomerang' shape, and regions of minimal curvature represent stable directional progression suitable for cache reuse.
Diffusion models possess a self-correction capability in early sampling stages, effectively filtering out cache-induced noise, which diminishes in later stages where denoising strength is weaker.

Cache-induced noise is correlated across sampling steps, and its derivative is linked to trajectory curvature, allowing for dynamic estimation and correction, further enhanced by frequency-domain filtering.
originalProblem: Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models are computationally intensive, requiring numerous sampling steps and complex computations per step, hindering efficient deployment.
Existing training-free cache reuse methods for DiTs rely on local similarity between adjacent steps, often leading to suboptimal cache placement and quality degradation, especially in later stages.
Noise introduced by cache reuse in later sampling stages of diffusion models is difficult to recover from, causing irreversible trajectory deviations and compromised generative quality.
results: OmniCache achieved a 2-2.5x speedup on models like OpenSora and Latte for video generation, with minimal performance degradation (e.g., 0.16% VBench drop for 2x speedup on OpenSora).

It demonstrated robust performance on a distilled CogVideoX-5b-I2V model with only 16 steps, yielding a 1.45x speedup with 'nearly lossless' quality, where other methods failed or caused model collapse.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the noise correction module and the frequency filtering components are crucial for maintaining high generative quality, with the full OmniCache achieving 'nearly lossless performance'.
solution: OmniCache analyzes the global sampling trajectory of DiTs, identifying optimal cache reuse points based on minimal trajectory curvature for stable directional progression.
It integrates a dynamic, frequency-aware noise correction mechanism that estimates and filters cache-induced noise to maintain generative quality across different sampling stages.

The method employs a two-stage process: an offline calibration to determine optimal reuse steps and noise correlation, and an online inference stage for acceleration with real-time noise estimation and correction.
paper_summary.summary: OmniCache, a training-free method from Zhipu AI and Nanjing University, accelerates Diffusion Transformer models by adopting a trajectory-oriented global perspective for cache reuse, moving beyond local similarity. It achieves up to 2.5x speedup with minimal quality degradation on standard models and maintains near-lossless quality even on low-redundancy distilled models where other methods fail.

title: OmniCache: A Trajectory-Oriented Global Perspective on Training-Free Cache Reuse for Diffusion Transformer Models

abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models enable high-quality image generation but are computationally expensive. While prior work optimizes per-inference efficiency, we explore an orthogonal approach: reducing redundancy across correlated prompts. Our method leverages the coarse-to-fine nature of diffusion models, where early denoising steps capture shared structures among similar prompts. We propose a training-free approach that clusters prompts based on semantic similarity and shares computation in early diffusion steps. Experiments show that for models trained conditioned on image embeddings, our approach significantly reduces compute cost while improving image quality. By leveraging UnClip's text-to-image prior, we enhance diffusion step allocation for greater efficiency. Our method seamlessly integrates with existing pipelines, scales with prompt sets, and reduces the environmental and financial burden of large-scale text-to-image generation. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Diffusion models generate images in a coarse-to-fine manner, establishing global structures in early denoising steps before adding fine details.
Semantically similar text prompts lead to highly redundant computations in these early, structure-defining denoising steps.
Diffusion models trained with text-to-image priors (e.g., Kandinsky) are particularly well-suited for compute sharing, as they exhibit a more gradual emergence of high-frequency details.
originalProblem: Text-to-image diffusion models are computationally expensive, posing significant financial and environmental burdens, especially for large-scale generation.
Existing optimization methods primarily focus on accelerating single-image generation, neglecting the common user workflow of generating extensive sets of semantically correlated images.
Generating sets of similar images with diffusion models involves redundant computation in early denoising steps, which establish coarse, shared visual structures.
results: The method achieved substantial computational savings, ranging from 50.43% on diverse datasets to up to 73.93% on structured prompt sets, compared to standard 40-step generation.
When compared at fixed, lower compute budgets (e.g., ~31 steps per image), Hierarchical Diffusion consistently produced higher VQA scores than standard diffusion using a full 40-step budget.
The approach is most effective with diffusion models trained with a text-to-image prior (e.g., Kandinsky), offering greater savings and improved quality due to their gradual detail emergence.
solution: A training-free method, Hierarchical Diffusion, is introduced to reuse computation across correlated text prompts for image generation.
It constructs a hierarchical embedding tree using agglomerative clustering on prompt embeddings, grouping semantically similar prompts.

During denoising, the method adaptively selects embeddings from this tree, utilizing averaged, more general embeddings in early steps and progressively more specific ones in later steps based on a heterogeneity score.
paper_summary.summary: The Hierarchical Diffusion method, developed by researchers at the University of Chicago and Adobe Research, reduces the computational cost of generating sets of text-to-image outputs by reusing early denoising steps across semantically related prompts. This training-free approach achieves 50-74% computational savings while maintaining or enhancing image quality compared to standard generation.

title: Reusing Computation in Text-to-Image Diffusion for Efficient Generation of Image Sets

abstract:
The Text-to-Video (T2V) model aims to generate dynamic and expressive videos
from textual prompts. The generation pipeline typically involves multiple
modules, such as language encoder, Diffusion Transformer (DiT), and Variational
Autoencoders (VAE). Existing serving systems often rely on monolithic model
deployment, while overlooking the distinct characteristics of each module,
leading to inefficient GPU utilization. In addition, DiT exhibits varying
performance gains across different resolutions and degrees of parallelism, and
significant optimization potential remains unexplored. To address these
problems, we present DDiT, a flexible system that integrates both inter-phase
and intra-phase optimizations. DDiT focuses on two key metrics: optimal degree
of parallelism, which prevents excessive parallelism for specific resolutions,
and starvation time, which quantifies the sacrifice of each request. To this
end, DDiT introduces a decoupled control mechanism to minimize the
computational inefficiency caused by imbalances in the degree of parallelism
between the DiT and VAE phases. It also designs a greedy resource allocation
algorithm with a novel scheduling mechanism that operates at the single-step
granularity, enabling dynamic and timely resource scaling. Our evaluation on
the T5 encoder, OpenSora SDDiT, and OpenSora VAE models across diverse datasets
reveals that DDiT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by up to
1.44x in p99 latency and 1.43x in average latency.

organization_info.name: Institute of Mathematics and Interdisciplinary Sciences, Xidian University

title: DDiT: Dynamic Resource Allocation for Diffusion Transformer Model  Serving

abstract:
We present Cornserve, an efficient online serving system for an emerging class of multimodal models called Any-to-Any models. Any-to-Any models accept combinations of text and multimodal data (e.g., image, video, audio) as input and also generate combinations of text and multimodal data as output, introducing request type, computation path, and computation scaling heterogeneity in model serving.
Cornserve allows model developers to describe the computation graph of generic Any-to-Any models, which consists of heterogeneous components such as multimodal encoders, autoregressive models like Large Language Models (LLMs), and multimodal generators like Diffusion Transformers (DiTs). Given this, Cornserve's planner automatically finds an optimized deployment plan for the model, including whether and how to disaggregate the model into smaller components based on model and workload characteristics. Cornserve's distributed runtime then executes the model per the plan, efficiently handling Any-to-Any model heterogeneity during online serving. Evaluations show that Cornserve can efficiently serve diverse Any-to-Any models and workloads, delivering up to 3.81$\times$ throughput improvement and up to 5.79$\times$ tail latency reduction over existing solutions.

keyInsights: The optimal deployment strategy for multimodal models is highly dynamic, varying significantly with both the model's architecture and the characteristics of the incoming workload.
Abstracting GPU allocation into fixed-size "cells" allows for tractable offline planning and efficient online adaptation of deployment strategies.
A unique record-and-replay mechanism enables handling arbitrary Python control flow within multimodal model definitions, providing flexibility for model developers.
originalProblem: Any-to-Any multimodal models exhibit high heterogeneity in computation paths, as requests activate diverse combinations of encoders, LLMs, and generators.
These models also have heterogeneous computation scaling, with components like image encoders and diffusion transformers having vastly different resource demands.
Existing serving systems are specialized and employ fixed deployment strategies, which are often suboptimal for the varying architectures and workloads of Any-to-Any models.
results: Achieved up to 3.81x throughput improvement and 5.79x reduction in P99 latency for Qwen 2.5 Omni 7B compared to monolithic deployments.
Successfully served Qwen 3 Omni 30B, a model that failed on monolithic deployments due to GPU Out-Of-Memory errors, demonstrating the necessity of its disaggregation strategy.
Demonstrated that request-static routing provides 3.7x higher throughput than dynamic load balancing in an InternVL 3 scenario.
solution: Cornserve introduces a planner that leverages a "cell abstraction" to perform efficient offline precomputation of deployment strategies, followed by rapid online adaptation to changing workloads.
Its distributed runtime supports flexible Python-based APIs for model definition and utilizes a record-and-replay mechanism to execute dynamic computation graphs for each request.
The system implements request-static routing, where the planner pre-determines optimal execution paths for different request types, minimizing runtime overhead.
paper_summary.summary: Cornserve is a unified serving system designed to efficiently deploy Any-to-Any multimodal models, which can process and generate diverse data types like text, images, and audio. It adaptively optimizes resource allocation and deployment strategies based on model architecture and workload characteristics, leading to significant throughput and latency improvements.

title: Cornserve: Efficiently Serving Any-to-Any Multimodal Models

abstract:
Text-to-image generation using diffusion models has seen explosive popularity
owing to their ability in producing high quality images adhering to text
prompts. However, production-grade diffusion model serving is a resource
intensive task that not only require high-end GPUs which are expensive but also
incurs considerable latency. In this paper, we introduce a technique called
approximate-caching that can reduce such iterative denoising steps for an image
generation based on a prompt by reusing intermediate noise states created
during a prior image generation for similar prompts. Based on this idea, we
present an end to end text-to-image system, Nirvana, that uses the
approximate-caching with a novel cache management-policy Least Computationally
Beneficial and Frequently Used (LCBFU) to provide % GPU compute savings, 19.8%
end-to-end latency reduction and 19% dollar savings, on average, on two real
production workloads. We further present an extensive characterization of real
production text-to-image prompts from the perspective of caching, popularity
and reuse of intermediate states in a large production environment.

keyInsights: Reusing and reconditioning intermediate noise states from prior diffusion processes, rather than full images, allows for substantial computational savings without compromising image quality for new, semantically similar prompts.
Real-world text-to-image prompts exhibit high similarity both within and across user sessions, providing a strong basis for approximate caching to achieve high cache hit rates.

A cache eviction policy that considers both access frequency and the potential computational benefit (skipped steps) of cached items significantly outperforms traditional policies in terms of overall compute savings.
originalProblem: Serving state-of-the-art text-to-image diffusion models is computationally intensive, leading to high GPU costs and significant inference latency (e.g., 50-100 denoising steps).

Existing solutions either compromise generated image quality (e.g., smaller models) or incur prohibitive costs (e.g., powerful GPUs), or fail to adapt retrieved content to new prompts (pure retrieval systems).
The iterative nature of diffusion models, even with algorithmic optimizations, remains a bottleneck for real-time interactive applications and large-scale deployments.
results: NIRVANA reduced GPU computation time by 21% and end-to-end latency by 19.8%, leading to a 19% amortized dollar saving and a 27% increase in system throughput.

Generated image quality remained comparable to the unoptimized (VANILLA) diffusion model, significantly outperforming pure retrieval-based systems and smaller diffusion models on FID, CLIP Score, and PickScore metrics.
The LCBFU cache policy achieved up to 23% compute savings, demonstrating superior efficiency compared to LRU (20%), LFU (19%), and FIFO (17%) policies while maintaining consistent image quality.
solution: NIRVANA introduces approximate caching, which reuses intermediate noise states from previous generations as a starting point, reducing the number of required denoising steps for new prompts.
The system dynamically selects the optimal number of skipped steps (K) based on prompt similarity to maintain image quality through a reconditioning process specific to the new input prompt.

A novel cache management policy, LCBFU (Least Computationally Beneficial and Frequently Used), prioritizes retaining cached items that offer the most significant computational savings for effective resource utilization.
paper_summary.summary: Adobe Research developed NIRVANA, a system that uses approximate caching of intermediate noise states for text-to-image diffusion models, enabling a 21% reduction in GPU computation time and a 19.8% decrease in end-to-end latency while preserving image quality comparable to unoptimized models.

title: Approximate Caching for Efficiently Serving Diffusion Models

abstract:
Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based video diffusion models generate
high-quality videos at scale but incur prohibitive processing latency and
memory costs for long videos. To address this, we propose a novel distributed
inference strategy, termed DualParal. The core idea is that, instead of
generating an entire video on a single GPU, we parallelize both temporal frames
and model layers across GPUs. However, a naive implementation of this division
faces a key limitation: since diffusion models require synchronized noise
levels across frames, this implementation leads to the serialization of
original parallelisms. We leverage a block-wise denoising scheme to handle
this. Namely, we process a sequence of frame blocks through the pipeline with
progressively decreasing noise levels. Each GPU handles a specific block and
layer subset while passing previous results to the next GPU, enabling
asynchronous computation and communication. To further optimize performance, we
incorporate two key enhancements. Firstly, a feature cache is implemented on
each GPU to store and reuse features from the prior block as context,
minimizing inter-GPU communication and redundant computation. Secondly, we
employ a coordinated noise initialization strategy, ensuring globally
consistent temporal dynamics by sharing initial noise patterns across GPUs
without extra resource costs. Together, these enable fast, artifact-free, and
infinitely long video generation. Applied to the latest diffusion transformer
video generator, our method efficiently produces 1,025-frame videos with up to
6.54$\times$ lower latency and 1.48$\times$ lower memory cost on 8$\times$RTX
4090 GPUs.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

title: Minute-Long Videos with Dual Parallelisms

abstract: Large language model (LLM) serving demands low latency and high throughput, but high load variability makes it challenging to achieve high GPU utilization. In this paper, we identify a synergetic but overlooked opportunity to co-serve latency-critical online requests alongside latency-tolerant offline tasks such as model benchmarking. While promising, existing serving systems fail to co-serve them efficiently, as their coarse-grained resource management at the request or iteration level cannot harvest millisecond-level GPU idle cycles without introducing interference that violates online latency objectives. ConServe is a new LLM co-serving system that achieves high throughput and strong online latency guarantees by managing resources at finer granularities. ConServe introduces three techniques: (1) a latency-aware token-level scheduler that precisely sizes offline batches and tokens to fit within online latency objectives; (2) sub-iteration, layer-wise preemption that allows offline tasks to yield to online load spikes; and (3) incremental KV cache management that enables preempting and resuming offline requests at near-zero cost. Evaluations with Llama-3.1 and Qwen-2.5 models on real-world workloads show that ConServe delivers an average of 2.2$\times$ higher throughput and reduces online serving tail latency by 2.9$\times$ on average compared to state-of-the-art systems.

keyInsights: Achieving efficient co-serving of online and offline LLM workloads requires fine-grained resource management and execution orchestration at the sub-request and sub-iteration levels.
A dynamic performance model that considers context length and KV cache pressure is essential for predictable online latency when sharing GPU resources.
Decoupling KV cache state management to the token level allows for practical, low-overhead preemption and resumption of large offline inference tasks without significant performance degradation.
originalProblem: Large language model serving infrastructure suffers from severe GPU underutilization due to bursty online inference workloads, necessitating over-provisioning for peak demand.
Existing LLM serving systems manage resources at coarse request or iteration granularities, leading to unpredictable online latency and inefficient execution of co-located offline tasks.
Current preemption mechanisms incur high overheads, either by forcing costly recomputation of the entire KV cache or stalling the GPU with slow host memory swaps.
results: ConServe yielded 2.2 times higher offline throughput and reduced P99 online serving tail latency by an average of 2.9 times when compared to state-of-the-art baselines like Sarathi-Preemptive and DistServe-Preemptive.
Online latency (P99 TTFT and TBT) was maintained remarkably close to the online-only baseline, typically within 25% and 19% respectively, across varied request rates and models.

The system achieved an average of 82.3% of the optimal offline throughput, demonstrating up to 7.48 times higher offline throughput than Sarathi-P by eliminating recomputation overhead, and showed strong resilience to bursty workloads.
solution: ConServe implements SLO-aware token-level scheduling using a context-aware performance model to dynamically determine the maximum offline tokens that can be added to a batch without violating online latency SLOs.

It introduces layer-wise preemption by instrumenting LLMs with lightweight safepoints between Transformer layers, enabling rapid preemption (within milliseconds) of offline tasks upon online load spikes.

The system utilizes incremental KV cache management, asynchronously checkpointing and prefetching token-level KV cache pages to amortize I/O costs and eliminate recomputation during frequent preemptions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from UC Berkeley, UCLA, Rice University, UC Davis, and Intel developed ConServe, a system for co-serving latency-critical online and latency-tolerant offline LLM inference on shared GPUs. This approach achieves 2.2 times higher offline throughput and reduces P99 online serving tail latency by 2.9 times compared to existing systems, while maintaining strict online Service Level Objectives.

title: ConServe: Fine-Grained GPU Harvesting for LLM Online and Offline Co-Serving

abstract: Diffusion models have demonstrated a remarkable ability in Text-to-Image (T2I) generation applications. Despite the advanced generation output, they suffer from heavy computation overhead, especially for large models that contain tens of billions of parameters. Prior work has illustrated that replacing part of the denoising steps with a smaller model still maintains the generation quality. However, these methods only focus on saving computation for some timesteps, ignoring the difference in compute demand within one timestep. In this work, we propose HybridStitch, a new T2I generation paradigm that treats generation like editing. Specifically, we introduce a hybrid stage that jointly incorporates both the large model and the small model. HybridStitch separates the entire image into two regions: one that is relatively easy to render, enabling an early transition to the smaller model, and another that is more complex and therefore requires refinement by the large model. HybridStitch employs the small model to construct a coarse sketch while exploiting the large model to edit and refine the complex regions. According to our evaluation, HybridStitch achieves 1.83$\times$ speedup on Stable Diffusion 3, which is faster than all existing mixture of model methods.

keyInsights: Image regions exhibit heterogeneous computational demands during denoising, allowing for selective application of large and small models to optimize resource use.
The L1 distance between latent states at successive timesteps serves as an effective metric for adaptively determining when to transition between different model configurations.
Maintaining global context via KV cache reuse for masked regions is crucial for preserving image quality when only a subset of tokens is processed by the larger model.
originalProblem: High computational overhead and inference latency of large Text-to-Image (T2I) diffusion models hinder their deployment in latency-sensitive applications.

Existing "Mixture of Models" acceleration techniques switch between models at the entire image granularity, overlooking heterogeneous computational demands within different image regions at a single denoising step.
This uniform switching leads to either quality degradation in complex regions due to premature switching or unnecessary latency from using a large model on simpler regions.
results: Achieved a speedup of up to 1.83x over the pure large model (Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large), outperforming T-Stitch (1.41x) and SRDiffusion (1.55x) in efficiency.

Demonstrated improved image quality with a best Frechet Inception Distance (FID) of 30.34 and significantly lower Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) scores (0.35-0.42) compared to baselines.
Qualitative results showed no noticeable degradation in visual quality at the highest speedup, and the dynamic masking strategy proved essential for maintaining quality over static masking.
solution: HybridStitch proposes a three-stage, region-aware model stitching paradigm, treating image generation as an editing process with adaptive pixel and timestep level model transitions.
It utilizes a dynamic mask in the hybrid stage, applying a large model only to identified "difficult" regions while a smaller model processes the entire latent state for global consistency.

A Key and Value (KV) cache from the previous denoising step is leveraged to provide global context for the large model's masked attention computations, ensuring quality preservation without full-image processing.
paper_summary.summary: HybridStitch accelerates Text-to-Image diffusion models by employing a region-aware model stitching technique, dynamically switching between a large and a small model at pixel and timestep levels during denoising. The method achieves up to a 1.83x speedup over the pure large model, reducing latency by 18.06% compared to SRDiffusion, while improving image quality with a best FID of 30.34 and significantly lower LPIPS scores of 0.35-0.42.

title: HybridStitch: Pixel and Timestep Level Model Stitching for Diffusion Acceleration

abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models have achieved remarkable visual quality but incur high computational costs, making latency-aware, scalable deployment challenging. To address this, we advocate a hybrid architecture that achieves query awareness when serving diffusion models. Unlike existing query-aware serving systems that cascade lightweight and heavyweight models with a fixed configuration, our hybrid architecture first routes each query directly to a suitable model variant, then reroutes it to a cascaded heavyweight model only if necessary. We theoretically analyze conditions for the hybrid architecture to outperform non-hybrid alternatives in latency and response quality. Building on this architecture, we design HADIS, a hybrid serving system for latency-aware diffusion models that jointly optimizes cascade model selection, query routing, and resource allocation. To reduce the complexity of resource management, HADIS uses an offline profiling phase to produce a Pareto-optimal cascade configuration table. At runtime, HADIS selects the best cascade configuration and GPU allocation given latency and workload constraints. Empirical evaluations on real-world traces demonstrate that HADIS improves response quality by up to 35% while reducing latency violation rates by 2.7-45$\times$ compared to state-of-the-art model serving systems.
title: HADIS: Hybrid Adaptive Diffusion Model Serving for Efficient Text-to-Image Generation

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) extend LLMs to handle images, videos, and audio by incorporating feature extractors and projection modules. However, these additional components -- combined with complex inference pipelines and heterogeneous workloads -- introduce significant inference overhead. Therefore, efficiently serving MLLMs remains a major challenge. Current tightly coupled serving architectures struggle to distinguish between mixed request types or adapt parallelism strategies to different inference stages, leading to increased time-to-first-token (TTFT) latency and poor resource utilization. To address this, we introduce Elastic Multimodal Parallelism (EMP), a new serving paradigm that elastically adapts to resource heterogeneity across request types and inference stages. Building upon EMP, we develop ElasticMM, an MLLM serving system that (1) separates requests into independent modality groups with dynamic resource allocation via a modality-aware load balancer; (2) decouples inference stages and enables parallelism adjustment and adaptive scaling via elastic partition scheduling; and (3) improves inference efficiency through unified multimodal prefix caching and non-blocking encoding. Experiments on diverse real-world datasets show that ElasticMM outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) serving systems, reducing TTFT by up to 4.2x and achieving 3.2-4.5x higher throughput while meeting service-level objectives (SLOs).

organization_info.name: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

title: ElasticMM: Efficient Multimodal LLMs Serving with Elastic Multimodal Parallelism

abstract: Diffusion pipelines, renowned for their powerful visual generation capabilities, have seen widespread adoption in generative vision tasks (e.g., text-to-image/video). These pipelines typically follow an encode--diffuse--decode three-stage architecture. Current serving systems deploy diffusion pipelines within a static, manual, and pipeline-level paradigm, allocating the same resources to every request and stage. However, through an in-depth analysis, we find that such a paradigm is inefficient due to the discrepancy in resource needs across the three stages of each request, as well as across different requests. Following the analysis, we propose the dynamic stage-level serving paradigm and develop TridentServe, a brand new diffusion serving system. TridentServe automatically, dynamically derives the placement plan (i.e., how each stage resides) for pipeline deployment and the dispatch plan (i.e., how the requests are routed) for request processing, co-optimizing the resource allocation for both model and requests. Extensive experiments show that TridentServe consistently improves SLO attainment and reduces average/P95 latencies by up to 2.5x and 3.6x/4.1x over existing works across a variety of workloads.

keyInsights: Diffusion Pipelines exhibit profound heterogeneity across their stages (Encode, Diffuse, Decode) and also across diverse user requests, which current serving systems fail to address efficiently.

Dynamic, fine-grained resource management at the stage-level, coupled with co-optimization of model placement and request dispatching, is crucial for maximizing efficiency and robustness for generative vision tasks.
Seamless, no-downtime model re-deployment ("Adjust-on-Dispatch") and proactive tensor push mechanisms are essential for adaptive serving without incurring significant overheads.
originalProblem: Current serving systems for Diffusion Pipelines fail to account for the substantial heterogeneity in computational and memory demands across different model stages (Encode, Diffuse, Decode).
Existing systems also struggle with diverse user requests (e.g., varying resolutions, video durations) and the dynamic, unpredictable nature of real-world generative vision task workloads.
Traditional static, pipeline-level resource allocation leads to severe inefficiencies, resource underutilization, and Out-Of-Memory errors, especially for larger, more complex generative models.
results: TridentServe achieved up to 6.6x higher Service Level Objective (SLO) attainment and up to 4.8x reduction in P95 latency compared to static pipeline-level baselines.
It eliminated Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors encountered by static and pipeline-level baselines when serving large Diffusion models like Flux and HunyuanVideo.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical roles of dynamic placement switching, stage-level resource allocation, and the resource-aware dispatcher in achieving performance gains, particularly under dynamic workloads.
solution: TridentServe introduces a dynamic, stage-level serving paradigm by disaggregating Diffusion Pipelines into their constituent stages and managing resources at this fine-grained level.

It uses a Planning-Execution architecture with a Dynamic Orchestrator to generate adaptive Placement Plans for model deployment and a Resource-Aware Dispatcher for Dispatch Plans to process individual requests.

The system co-optimizes model placement (minimizing inter-stage communication, balancing processing speeds) and request dispatching (maximizing SLO attainment via an ILP solver) based on offline profiling and real-time monitoring.
paper_summary.summary: Peking University researchers developed TridentServe, a dynamic, stage-level serving system for Diffusion Pipelines that intelligently adapts resource allocation. The system improves Service Level Objective attainment by up to 6.6x and reduces P95 latency by up to 4.8x compared to static pipeline-level baselines, while eliminating Out-Of-Memory errors for large generative models.

title: TridentServe: A Stage-level Serving System for Diffusion Pipelines

abstract:
Generative image editing using diffusion models has become a prevalent
application in today's AI cloud services. In production environments, image
editing typically involves a mask that specifies the regions of an image
template to be edited. The use of masks provides direct control over the
editing process and introduces sparsity in the model inference. In this paper,
we present InstGenIE, a system that efficiently serves image editing requests.
The key insight behind InstGenIE is that image editing only modifies the masked
regions of image templates while preserving the original content in the
unmasked areas. Driven by this insight, InstGenIE judiciously skips redundant
computations associated with the unmasked areas by reusing cached intermediate
activations from previous inferences. To mitigate the high cache loading
overhead, InstGenIE employs a bubble-free pipeline scheme that overlaps
computation with cache loading. Additionally, to reduce queuing latency in
online serving while improving the GPU utilization, InstGenIE proposes a novel
continuous batching strategy for diffusion model serving, allowing newly
arrived requests to join the running batch in just one step of denoising
computation, without waiting for the entire batch to complete. As heterogeneous
masks induce imbalanced loads, InstGenIE also develops a load balancing
strategy that takes into account the loads of both computation and cache
loading. Collectively, InstGenIE outperforms state-of-the-art diffusion serving
systems for image editing, achieving up to 3x higher throughput and reducing
average request latency by up to 14.7x while ensuring image quality.

keyInsights: Most image editing operations are sparse, affecting only small regions, which allows for caching and reusing intermediate activations from unchanged areas.
Carefully overlapping computation with cache loading operations can eliminate idle time in the execution pipeline.
Adapting continuous batching techniques from large language models can improve throughput and reduce latency for diffusion models by allowing new requests to join ongoing batches dynamically.
Load balancing should account for heterogeneous computational demands of different editing requests, considering both computation and cache loading costs.
originalProblem: Diffusion models incur high computational load and inefficient resource utilization when serving image editing requests at scale.
Varying sizes of editing masks lead to load imbalance and can cause long queuing times.
Naive caching of intermediate activations can introduce significant latency penalties due to loading overhead.
results: Achieved up to 3x throughput improvement over state-of-the-art systems.
Reduced average latency by up to 14.7x and tail latency (P95) by up to 29%.
Maintained image quality, confirmed by CLIP, FID, and SSIM metrics, across various diffusion models including Stable Diffusion 2.1, SDXL, and Flux.
solution: Introduces mask-aware computation that selectively processes only masked regions and reuses cached activations for unmasked areas.
Implements a bubble-free pipeline scheme that overlaps computation with cache loading operations to minimize latency.
Adapts continuous batching and CPU-GPU disaggregation to improve GPU utilization and reduce queuing times for diffusion model serving.
Deploys mask-aware load balancing using predictive models to distribute requests efficiently across worker replicas.
paper_summary.summary: InstGenIE is a system optimization approach for generative image editing that leverages the sparse nature of editing operations to significantly improve the efficiency of diffusion models in production. By combining mask-aware computation, advanced pipelining, and continuous batching, the system achieves up to 3x higher throughput and up to 14.7x lower average latency while preserving image quality.

title: InstGenIE: Generative Image Editing Made Efficient with Mask-aware  Caching and Scheduling

abstract:
Deploying multiple models within shared GPU clusters is promising for improving resource efficiency in large language model (LLM) serving. Existing multi-LLM serving systems optimize GPU utilization at the cost of worse inference performance, especially time-to-first-token (TTFT). We identify the root cause of such compromise as their unawareness of future workload characteristics. In contrast, recent analysis on real-world traces has shown the high periodicity and long-term predictability of LLM serving workloads.
We propose universal GPU workers to enable one-for-many GPU prewarming that loads models with knowledge of future workloads. Based on universal GPU workers, we design and build WarmServe, a multi-LLM serving system that (1) mitigates cluster-wide prewarming interference by adopting an evict-aware model placement strategy, (2) prepares universal GPU workers in advance by proactive prewarming, and (3) manages GPU memory with a zero-overhead memory switching mechanism. Evaluation under real-world datasets shows that WarmServe improves TTFT by up to 50.8$\times$ compared to the state-of-the-art autoscaling-based system, while being capable of serving up to 2.5$\times$ more requests compared to the GPU-sharing system.

keyInsights: Multi-LLM serving workloads, despite short-term bursts, exhibit high periodicity and long-term predictability, which can be effectively exploited for proactive resource provisioning.
Combining dedicated resources for active inference with shared, proactive prewarming can simultaneously achieve low Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) and high Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT).
A "one-for-many" prewarming strategy, where a single GPU can store weights for multiple potential models, significantly enhances resource utilization for cold-start mitigation.
originalProblem: Existing multi-LLM serving systems incur significant cold start latency (TTFT) with autoscaling due to the necessity of loading large model checkpoints.
GPU sharing solutions, while avoiding cold starts, degrade inference performance (TPOT) due to resource contention and limited KV cache for colocated models.
